result.markdown:
# More Bayesian Analyis of a Numerical Variable {#sec-numerical-skew} ::: {#exm-partytime-prior} How late do people arrive at parties? [FiveThirtyEight](https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/how-to-estimate-when-people-will-arrive-at-a-party/) conducted a survey to address this question. We'll assume this is a reasonably representative sample and arrival times are measured reliably. Arrival times are measured in minutes after the scheduled start time, rounded to the nearest minute. (Negative arrival times represent arrivals before the scheduled start time.) ::: 1. What would you expect the population distribution of party arrival times to look like? For example, what percent of arrivals would you expect to be early (negative)? Later than two hours? What would you expect of other features like shape, center, and variability? \ \ \ \ \ 1. We will start by assuming that, given the values of relevant parameters, arrival times follow a conditional Normal distribution. Describe what this assumption says about arrival times. What are the relevant parameters, and what do they represent? (We will revisit this conditional Normal assumption later, but go with it for now.) \ \ \ \ \ 1. Suggest a prior distribution. Explain your reasoning. \ \ \ \ \ 1. Describe how you could simulate the prior predictive distribution. \ \ \ \ \ 1. This [applet](https://kevin-davisross.shinyapps.io/Normal-Prior-Predictive/) conducts the simulation from the previous part. Move the sliders to enter your prior mean and SD for $\mu$ and $\sigma$. Does the distribution of arrival times seem reasonable based on your expectations? For example, what percent of arrivals are early (negative), or later than two hours --- and do these values seem reasonable? If not, revise your assumptions and try again (i.e., play with the sliders in the applet) until you find a distribution that is reasonable. Do not worry about getting it perfect; you just want to settle on assumptions that provide a reasonable starting point. (The assumptions include both the conditional Normal model for arrival times and the prior distribution of parameters. For now we are focusing on the prior distribution, so take the conditional Normal model as given. We'll revisit that assumption later.) \ \ \ \ \ ::: {#exm-partytime-posterior} @sec-partytime-data summarizes the FiveThirtyEight sample^[We couldn't find the raw FiveThirtyEight data online, so this data was constructed to match the histogram in the article.] of 803 arrival times (rounded to the nearest minute). We should always first explore the sample data, but to illustrate a point we will not do that yet. Instead, we'll skip straight to fitting the model. ::: 1. Use `brms` to fit the model and summarize the posterior distribution. Briefly describe the posterior distribution. \ \ \ \ \ 1. Simulate the posterior predictive distribution. Briefly describe the posterior predictive distribution. \ \ \ \ \ 1. Perform a visual posterior predictive check. What does the posterior predictive check say about the appropriateness of our model? Could any problems be fixed by switching from a Normal to a $t$ likelihood? \ \ \ \ \ ::: {#exm-partytime-posterior-skew} The sample data suggests a model that allows for skewness would be more appropriate than Normal. This primarily results in a change of the likelihood function. There are several likelihood functions we can use, but we'll try a [Skew Normal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Skew_normal_distribution) family, which introduces an additional skewness parameter $\alpha$. Since $\alpha$ is a parameter it also needs a prior distribution. Rather than redoing our prior predictive tuning, we'll rely on the fact that software packages like `brms` can utilize default priors. ::: 1. Use `brms` to fit the skew Normal model, letting `brms` choose the prior for $\alpha$ (but specifying the priors for $\mu$ and $\sigma$ as before). What prior does `brms` use? \ \ \ \ \ 1. Summarize and describe the posterior distribution. \ \ \ \ \ 1. Use simulation to approximate the posterior predictive distribution. Summarize and describe the posterior predictive distribution. \ \ \ \ \ 1. Perform a visual posterior predictive check. What does the posterior predictive check say about the appropriateness of the model? Which model seems to be a better fit for the data: likelihood based on Normal or Skew-Normal? \ \ \ \ \ 1. Fit the model to the data in `brms` using the `skew_normal` family but letting `brms` choose the prior for all parameters. What prior does `brms` choose? Is the posterior sensitive to the choice of prior? \ \ \ \ \ 1. Are the posterior distributions of parameters skewed or relatively symmetric? Is the posterior predictive distribution skewed or relatively symmetric? \ \ \ \ \ 1. Write a few sentences summarizing the conclusions of the Bayesian model in context. \ \ \ \ \ ## Notes ### Prior predictive distribution (Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} n_rep = 10000 mu_sim = rnorm(n_rep, 40, sd = 7) sigma_sim = pmax(0.001, rnorm(n_rep, 20, 5)) y_sim = rnorm(n_rep, mu_sim, sigma_sim) ggplot(data.frame(y_sim), aes(x = y_sim)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), col = bayes_col["prior_predict"], fill = "white") + geom_density(col = bayes_col["prior_predict"], linewidth = 1) + labs(x = "Arrival time (y, minutes after start)") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Sample data {#sec-partytime-data} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} data = read_csv("party-time.csv") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Rows: 803 Columns: 1 ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," dbl (1): minutes ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} head(data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 1 minutes 1 -10 2 -12 3 -18 4 -21 5 -15 6 -9 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(data$minutes) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Min. 1st Qu. Median Mean 3rd Qu. Max. -30.00 21.00 58.00 65.48 101.00 261.00 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} sd(data$minutes) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 58.20041 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(data, aes(x = minutes)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50, col = "black", fill = "white") + geom_density(linewidth = 1) + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Posterior distribution (Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(brms) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'brms' was built under R version 4.3.3 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: Rcpp ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'Rcpp' was built under R version 4.3.3 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading 'brms' package (version 2.22.0). Useful instructions can be found by typing help('brms'). A more detailed introduction to the package is available through vignette('brms_overview'). ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'brms' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:stats': ar ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidybayes) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'tidybayes' was built under R version 4.3.3 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'tidybayes' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:brms': dstudent_t, pstudent_t, qstudent_t, rstudent_t ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} library(bayesplot) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: package 'bayesplot' was built under R version 4.3.3 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` This is bayesplot version 1.11.1 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` - Online documentation and vignettes at mc-stan.org/bayesplot ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` - bayesplot theme set to bayesplot::theme_default() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` * Does _not_ affect other ggplot2 plots ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` * See ?bayesplot_theme_set for details on theme setting ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'bayesplot' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:brms': rhat ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fit <- brm(data = data, family = gaussian, minutes ~ 1, prior = c(prior(normal(40, 7), class = Intercept), prior(normal(20, 5), class = sigma)), iter = 3500, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, refresh = 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Compiling Stan program... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Start sampling ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Family: gaussian Links: mu = identity; sigma = identity Formula: minutes ~ 1 Data: data (Number of observations: 803) Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 3500; warmup = 1000; thin = 1; total post-warmup draws = 10000 Regression Coefficients: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept 63.62 1.90 59.89 67.31 1.00 7925 6536 Further Distributional Parameters: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sigma 55.63 1.26 53.19 58.16 1.00 8029 5966 Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1). ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pairs(fit, off_diag_args = list(alpha = 0.1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} posterior = fit |> spread_draws(b_Intercept, sigma) |> rename(mu = b_Intercept) posterior |> head(10) |> kbl() |> kable_styling() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
.chain .iteration .draw mu sigma
1 1 1 65.02298 55.31455
1 2 2 60.92569 57.07551
1 3 3 67.84054 55.26640
1 4 4 64.99232 54.94991
1 5 5 62.73370 56.72687
1 6 6 63.22659 56.69350
1 7 7 64.23976 54.95613
1 8 8 64.38425 54.61429
1 9 9 61.26003 55.34205
1 10 10 61.87223 56.32483
````` ::: ::: ### Posterior predictive distribution (Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} n_rep = nrow(posterior) posterior <- posterior |> mutate(y_sim = rnorm(n_rep, mean = posterior$mu, sd = posterior$sigma)) posterior |> head(10) |> kbl() |> kable_styling() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
.chain .iteration .draw mu sigma y_sim
1 1 1 65.02298 55.31455 63.927464
1 2 2 60.92569 57.07551 24.429415
1 3 3 67.84054 55.26640 115.485023
1 4 4 64.99232 54.94991 -9.153019
1 5 5 62.73370 56.72687 70.348386
1 6 6 63.22659 56.69350 70.117555
1 7 7 64.23976 54.95613 46.968762
1 8 8 64.38425 54.61429 10.376603
1 9 9 61.26003 55.34205 39.564128
1 10 10 61.87223 56.32483 94.967298
````` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(posterior, aes(x = y_sim)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"], fill = "white", bins = 100) + geom_density(linewidth = 1, col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"]) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes after start)") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # plot the cdf ggplot(posterior, aes(x = y_sim)) + stat_ecdf(col = bayes_col["prior_predict"], linewidth = 1) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes after start)", y = "cdf") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} quantile(posterior$y_sim, c(0.005, 0.995)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0.5% 99.5% -80.72404 205.14377 ``` ::: ::: ### Posterior predictive checking (Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pp_check(fit, ndraw = 100) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pp_check(fit, ndraw = 100, type = "ecdf_overlay") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Posterior distribution (Skew-Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fit <- brm(data = data, family = skew_normal, minutes ~ 1, prior = c(prior(normal(40, 7), class = Intercept), prior(normal(20, 5), class = sigma)), iter = 3500, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, refresh = 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Compiling Stan program... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Start sampling ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prior_summary(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` prior class coef group resp dpar nlpar lb ub source normal(0, 4) alpha default normal(40, 7) Intercept user normal(20, 5) sigma 0 user ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Family: skew_normal Links: mu = identity; sigma = identity; alpha = identity Formula: minutes ~ 1 Data: data (Number of observations: 803) Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 3500; warmup = 1000; thin = 1; total post-warmup draws = 10000 Regression Coefficients: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept 61.30 1.76 57.90 64.75 1.00 6030 6494 Further Distributional Parameters: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sigma 54.75 1.31 52.27 57.40 1.00 5397 6267 alpha 5.19 0.68 3.99 6.66 1.00 6015 5680 Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1). ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pairs(fit, off_diag_args = list(alpha = 0.1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} posterior = fit |> spread_draws(b_Intercept, sigma, alpha) |> rename(mu = b_Intercept) posterior |> head(10) |> kbl() |> kable_styling() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
.chain .iteration .draw mu sigma alpha
1 1 1 61.14092 53.58990 5.141311
1 2 2 59.82107 55.95199 5.327699
1 3 3 61.04911 52.08854 4.153378
1 4 4 58.84429 55.56491 5.909929
1 5 5 59.31336 54.05318 6.806539
1 6 6 57.72592 55.37278 5.847652
1 7 7 60.52559 54.32741 5.343750
1 8 8 59.47829 55.14924 5.466411
1 9 9 61.26679 53.03494 5.763313
1 10 10 59.42221 54.83531 5.288244
````` ::: ::: ### Posterior predictive distribution (Skew-Normal likelihood) `posterior_predict` simulates a sample of size 803 for each of the 10000 simulated $(\mu, \sigma, \alpha)$ triples from the posterior distribution, resuling in a 10000 x 803 matrix. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y_predict = posterior_predict(fit) y_predict[1:5, 1:5] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] [1,] 95.76681 52.87998 19.36111 67.996502 55.8700750 [2,] 78.47290 129.68699 46.94753 238.718608 -0.9809806 [3,] 110.50418 17.12719 152.60890 21.829781 -0.4454336 [4,] 136.53967 98.71127 67.16218 -3.115918 69.4129385 [5,] 121.13833 80.83180 141.74894 69.710591 18.4639068 ``` ::: ::: We'll just select the first column to get 10000 simulated $y$ values. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y_predict = data.frame(y_sim = y_predict[, 1]) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(y_predict, aes(x = y_sim)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"], fill = "white", bins = 100) + geom_density(linewidth = 1, col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"]) + labs(x = "Predicted arrival time (minutes after start)") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # plot the cdf ggplot(y_predict, aes(x = y_sim)) + stat_ecdf(col = bayes_col["prior_predict"], linewidth = 1) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes after start)", y = "cdf") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} quantile(y_predict$y_sim, c(0.005, 0.995)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0.5% 99.5% -33.97992 239.61983 ``` ::: ::: ### Posterior predictive checking (Skew-Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pp_check(fit, ndraw = 100) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pp_check(fit, ndraw = 100, type = "ecdf_overlay") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Default `brms` priors (Skew-Normal likelihood) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fit <- brm(data = data, family = skew_normal(), minutes ~ 1, iter = 3500, warmup = 1000, chains = 4, refresh = 0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Compiling Stan program... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Start sampling ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prior_summary(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` prior class coef group resp dpar nlpar lb ub source normal(0, 4) alpha default student_t(3, 58, 57.8) Intercept default student_t(3, 0, 57.8) sigma 0 default ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summary(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Family: skew_normal Links: mu = identity; sigma = identity; alpha = identity Formula: minutes ~ 1 Data: data (Number of observations: 803) Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 3500; warmup = 1000; thin = 1; total post-warmup draws = 10000 Regression Coefficients: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept 65.23 2.08 61.37 69.47 1.00 5282 4964 Further Distributional Parameters: Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sigma 58.59 1.65 55.45 61.99 1.00 5142 5357 alpha 5.74 0.78 4.37 7.43 1.00 5634 4362 Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1). ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-35-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pairs(fit, off_diag_args = list(alpha = 0.1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-36-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pp_check(fit, ndraw = 100) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} y_predict = posterior_predict(fit) y_predict = data.frame(y_sim = y_predict[, 1]) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(y_predict, aes(x = y_sim)) + geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"], fill = "white", bins = 100) + geom_density(linewidth = 1, col = bayes_col["posterior_predict"]) + labs(x = "Predicted arrival time (minutes after start)") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-39-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # plot the cdf ggplot(y_predict, aes(x = y_sim)) + stat_ecdf(col = bayes_col["prior_predict"], linewidth = 1) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes after start)", y = "cdf") + theme_bw() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](numerical-skew_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-40-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} quantile(y_predict$y_sim, c(0.005, 0.995)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 0.5% 99.5% -32.32382 255.38655 ``` ::: ::: ### Code for Shiny app Here is some code for creating a rough [applet](https://kevin-davisross.shinyapps.io/Normal-Prior-Predictive/) that you can embed within an RMarkdown file by adding `runtime: shiny` to the YAML metadata; see [Shiny Documents](https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/shiny-documents.html). (This code is not evaluated; you would need to run it on your own.) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sliderInput("mu_prior_mean", "Prior mean of mu:", value = 10, min = -60, max = 120) sliderInput("mu_prior_sd", "Prior SD of mu:", value = 10, min = 5, max = 120) sliderInput("sigma_prior_mean", "Prior mean of sigma:", value = 10, min = 5, max = 120) sliderInput("sigma_prior_sd", "Prior SD of sigma:", value = 10, min = 5, max = 120) renderPlot({ n_rep = 10000 sim_prior = data.frame( mu = rnorm(n_rep, input$mu_prior_mean, input$mu_prior_sd), sigma = rgamma(n_rep, shape = input$sigma_prior_mean ^ 2 / input$sigma_prior_sd ^ 2, rate = input$sigma_prior_mean / input$sigma_prior_sd ^ 2)) %>% mutate(y_predict = rnorm(n_rep, mu, sigma)) p1 = sim_prior %>% ggplot(aes(x = y_predict)) + geom_density(col = bayes_col[4]) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes)", title = "Simulated prior predictive distribution of arrival times: Density") + scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-60, 180, by = 30), limits = c(-60, 180)) + theme_bw() p2 = sim_prior %>% ggplot(aes(x = y_predict)) + stat_ecdf(col = bayes_col[4]) + labs(x = "Arrival time (minutes)", y = "Cumulative probability", title = "Simulated prior predictive distribution of arrival times: CDF") + scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-60, 180, by = 30), limits = c(-60, 180)) + theme_bw() grid.arrange(p1, p2) }) ``` :::
